clinicalStem: A 31-year-old woman with a 5-year history of hypertension managed with enalapril presents for her first antenatal visit at 10 weeks gestation. Her blood pressure today is 148/94 mmHg. She is otherwise well and her booking bloods are normal. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate pharmacological action?
options: Continue enalapril and add a low-dose diuretic to achieve better control
Switch enalapril to labetalol and counsel her on fetal risks of ACE inhibitor exposure
Continue enalapril; it is safe in the first trimester only
Switch enalapril to amlodipine and reassure her that no fetal harm has occurred
Discontinue enalapril with no replacement and monitor blood pressure weekly
explanation: ACE inhibitors are contraindicated throughout pregnancy. First-trimester exposure is associated with cardiovascular and CNS malformations; second- and third-trimester use causes fetal renal dysplasia, oligohydramnios, and neonatal renal failure (the fetopathic syndrome). Labetalol is a first-line antihypertensive in pregnancy. Counselling on exposure timing and teratogenic risk is mandatory.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Adding a diuretic to an ACE inhibitor doubles the teratogenic risk and does not address the core problem of continuing a contraindicated drug.
This is the correct answer: switching to a safe agent (labetalol) while counselling on the risk of first-trimester exposure is the appropriate action.
ACE inhibitors are NOT safe in any trimester; first-trimester exposure carries its own risk of cardiac and CNS defects.
Amlodipine is an acceptable alternative, but the student must also counsel on risks, not merely reassure; labetalol is the preferred first-line agent per most guidelines.
Stopping antihypertensives without substitution in a woman with established hypertension risks uncontrolled hypertension, pre-eclampsia, and maternal stroke.
clinicalPearl: ACE inhibitors and ARBs are contraindicated in ALL trimesters; switch to labetalol, methyldopa, or nifedipine immediately on confirmed pregnancy.
keyClue: Patient is on enalapril (ACE inhibitor) and is now 10 weeks pregnant.
commonExamTrap: Believing ACE inhibitors are only harmful in the second/third trimester — they carry teratogenic risk in every trimester.
missedConcept: The fetopathic syndrome of ACE inhibitors and the absolute contraindication across all trimesters of pregnancy.

clinicalStem: A 34-year-old woman (BMI 32 kg/m²) presents at 26 weeks gestation. She has a family history of type 2 diabetes in her mother and a previous baby who weighed 4.6 kg at birth. A 75 g oral glucose tolerance test is performed. Fasting glucose is 5.3 mmol/L and 2-hour glucose is 8.4 mmol/L. Which of the following best describes this result and the next management step?
options: Normal OGTT — no further action required
Borderline result — repeat the OGTT in 4 weeks
Gestational diabetes confirmed — refer to joint diabetes-obstetric clinic and commence dietary advice
Impaired fasting glucose only — advise lifestyle modifications and retest postnatally
Type 2 diabetes mellitus — start insulin immediately
explanation: WHO/IADPSG criteria define gestational diabetes as fasting glucose ≥5.1 mmol/L OR 1-hour ≥10.0 mmol/L OR 2-hour ≥8.5 mmol/L. This patient has a fasting of 5.3 (≥5.1) and a 2-hour of 8.4 (borderline but fasting alone is diagnostic). GDM is confirmed; management begins with dietary optimisation in a joint clinic and may escalate to metformin or insulin if targets are not met.
wrongAnswerExplanations: The fasting glucose of 5.3 mmol/L alone meets the IADPSG threshold of ≥5.1 mmol/L, so this is not a normal result.
There is no role for repeating the OGTT; a single abnormal value on a 75 g OGTT is sufficient to diagnose GDM.
This is the correct answer: GDM is confirmed (fasting ≥5.1 mmol/L) and the standard pathway is referral to a joint diabetes-obstetric clinic with dietary intervention as first-line treatment.
While the fasting glucose is raised, this does not preclude a diagnosis of GDM — the full picture with multiple risk factors makes immediate action mandatory, not mere lifestyle advice.
Pre-existing type 2 diabetes would have been identified pre-pregnancy or at booking; GDM is the appropriate diagnosis here, and insulin is reserved for failure of dietary and metformin therapy.
clinicalPearl: Using IADPSG criteria, a single abnormal value on a 75 g OGTT (fasting ≥5.1, 1-hr ≥10.0, or 2-hr ≥8.5 mmol/L) is sufficient to diagnose gestational diabetes.
keyClue: Fasting glucose of 5.3 mmol/L on the 75 g OGTT at 26 weeks — meets the ≥5.1 mmol/L threshold.
commonExamTrap: Using the old non-pregnant thresholds (fasting ≥7.0) and calling this result normal.
missedConcept: The IADPSG fasting threshold for GDM is 5.1 mmol/L, lower than the standard diabetic threshold.

clinicalStem: A 22-year-old nulliparous woman presents 4 days after unprotected intercourse requesting emergency contraception. She is otherwise healthy, takes no regular medications, and has a regular 28-day cycle with her last menstrual period starting 10 days ago. She has no history of pelvic inflammatory disease. What is the most effective method of emergency contraception available to her now?
options: Levonorgestrel 1.5 mg orally — most effective option within 72 hours
Ulipristal acetate 30 mg orally — effective up to 5 days
Combined oral contraceptive pill in a Yuzpe regimen
Copper intrauterine device insertion — most effective option and effective up to 5 days
Progestogen-only pill commenced immediately
explanation: The copper IUD is the most effective form of emergency contraception available, with a failure rate of less than 0.1%, and it can be inserted up to 5 days after unprotected intercourse OR up to 5 days after the estimated date of ovulation. At day 10 of a 28-day cycle, ovulation has not yet occurred, so insertion is still within the eligible window. Levonorgestrel is only effective within 72 hours and ulipristal within 120 hours, but neither approaches the efficacy of the copper IUD.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Levonorgestrel is only licensed for use within 72 hours (3 days); this patient is at 96 hours post-intercourse, so levonorgestrel is outside its licensed window.
Ulipristal acetate is effective up to 120 hours (5 days) and is a good option, but it is less effective than the copper IUD, which remains the most effective emergency contraceptive overall.
The Yuzpe regimen is significantly less effective than modern dedicated emergency contraceptives and causes more side effects; it is rarely used today.
This is the correct answer: the copper IUD has the highest efficacy of all emergency contraceptive methods (<0.1% failure rate) and is within the valid time window here.
The progestogen-only pill is not licensed or effective as an emergency contraceptive when started after unprotected intercourse.
clinicalPearl: The copper IUD is the single most effective form of emergency contraception (failure rate <0.1%) and should be offered first when clinically appropriate, especially when the patient presents beyond 72 hours.
keyClue: Patient presents 4 days (96 hours) after unprotected intercourse — beyond the levonorgestrel window but within the copper IUD window.
commonExamTrap: Defaulting to levonorgestrel without checking the time frame; it is not licensed beyond 72 hours.
missedConcept: The copper IUD is more effective than any oral emergency contraceptive and has a wider time window.

clinicalStem: A 28-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes mellitus since age 12 attends a pre-conception counselling clinic. She is planning to conceive in the next 3 months. Her HbA1c is 58 mmol/mol (7.5%). She is currently taking insulin glargine and insulin aspart. She asks about folic acid supplementation. What dose of folic acid should she be prescribed, and for how long before conception?
options: 400 micrograms daily, starting 1 month before conception
400 micrograms daily, starting at a positive pregnancy test
5 mg daily, starting at least 3 months before conception
5 mg daily, starting only after the first missed period is confirmed
No folic acid is needed as she has no family history of neural tube defects
explanation: Women with pre-existing diabetes are at increased risk of neural tube defects (NTDs) due to the teratogenic effect of hyperglycaemia during early neurulation. They therefore require a higher dose of folic acid — 5 mg daily (high-dose) — commenced at least 3 months before conception and continued through the first trimester. The standard 400 microgram dose is insufficient for this high-risk group.
wrongAnswerExplanations: The 400 microgram dose is the standard recommendation for low-risk women; women with diabetes are high-risk and require 5 mg daily.
Folic acid must be started before conception to protect the neural tube, which closes by day 28 post-fertilisation — before most women know they are pregnant.
This is the correct answer: 5 mg folic acid daily started at least 3 months pre-conceptually is the recommendation for women with pre-existing diabetes.
Waiting until after a missed period is too late; the neural tube closes by day 28 of embryonic development, often before pregnancy is confirmed.
The risk of NTDs in maternal diabetes is not related to family history — hyperglycaemia itself is the teratogen, making high-dose folic acid mandatory regardless of family history.
clinicalPearl: Women with pre-existing diabetes need 5 mg folic acid daily (not 400 mcg) for at least 3 months before conception to reduce the substantially elevated risk of neural tube defects.
keyClue: Pre-existing type 1 diabetes — a recognised high-risk condition for NTDs requiring high-dose folic acid.
commonExamTrap: Prescribing the standard 400 mcg dose used for low-risk women instead of the 5 mg high-dose for diabetics.
missedConcept: Maternal diabetes significantly increases NTD risk; the required folic acid dose is 5 mg, not 400 mcg.

subtopic: NSAID use in the third trimester
clinicalStem: A 30-year-old woman at 34 weeks gestation presents to the emergency department with a painful, swollen right knee following a minor fall. She has no medical history and takes no regular medications. She rates her pain 7/10. Musculoskeletal examination suggests a soft tissue injury. The emergency physician considers analgesic options. Which of the following analgesics should be avoided specifically because of its effects on the fetus at this gestational age?
options: Paracetamol 1 g orally
Codeine phosphate 30 mg orally
Diclofenac 50 mg orally
Oral morphine 5 mg as a single dose
Topical ibuprofen gel applied to the knee
explanation: NSAIDs (including diclofenac) are contraindicated from 30 weeks gestation onwards due to the risk of premature closure of the ductus arteriosus, leading to fetal pulmonary hypertension. After 30 weeks, prostaglandin-mediated patency of the ductus arteriosus is critical; NSAID-mediated inhibition of prostaglandin synthesis can cause irreversible closure. Topical NSAIDs carry similar systemic absorption risks in late pregnancy. Paracetamol remains the safest analgesic throughout pregnancy.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Paracetamol is the safest analgesic in pregnancy at all stages; it has no specific contraindication at 34 weeks.
Codeine should be used cautiously (especially near term due to neonatal respiratory depression), but it is not the drug with the specific structural cardiac fetal risk at 34 weeks.
This is the correct answer: diclofenac (an NSAID) is contraindicated from 30 weeks due to premature closure of the ductus arteriosus and resultant fetal pulmonary hypertension.
Opioids carry risks (neonatal withdrawal, respiratory depression at delivery) but do not carry the specific ductus arteriosus risk; a single dose under monitoring is not absolutely contraindicated.
Topical NSAIDs are also contraindicated from 30 weeks as significant systemic absorption can still cause premature ductus arteriosus closure.
clinicalPearl: NSAIDs are contraindicated from 30 weeks gestation due to the risk of premature closure of the ductus arteriosus — this applies to both oral and topical formulations.
keyClue: 34 weeks gestation + NSAID consideration — the ductus arteriosus risk window (after 30 weeks).
commonExamTrap: Assuming topical NSAIDs are safe because absorption is lower — they still carry the same fetal risk in the third trimester.
missedConcept: The prostaglandin-dependent ductus arteriosus is vulnerable to NSAID closure from 30 weeks onwards.

subtopic: Combined OCP and migraine with aura
clinicalStem: A 26-year-old woman presents requesting reliable contraception. She experiences migraines with visual aura approximately once per month. She is a non-smoker and her blood pressure is 118/74 mmHg. She has no other medical history and her BMI is 23 kg/m². She has been using condoms but wants something more reliable. Which of the following is the most appropriate contraceptive recommendation?
options: Combined oral contraceptive pill (30 mcg ethinylestradiol / levonorgestrel)
Progestogen-only pill (desogestrel 75 mcg daily)
Combined contraceptive patch (ethinylestradiol / norelgestromin)
Combined vaginal ring (ethinylestradiol / etonogestrel)
Combined oral contraceptive pill with low oestrogen (20 mcg ethinylestradiol)
explanation: Migraine with aura is classified as WHO Medical Eligibility Criteria Category 4 (absolute contraindication) for all combined hormonal contraceptives (CHC), regardless of the oestrogen dose, formulation, or route of delivery. Oestrogen-containing methods increase the risk of ischaemic stroke in women with migraine with aura. The progestogen-only pill (e.g., desogestrel) is safe (WHO Category 2) and provides highly effective contraception without this risk.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Any combined oral contraceptive pill is WHO Category 4 (absolute contraindication) in migraine with aura; this option is unsafe regardless of the oestrogen dose.
This is the correct answer: the progestogen-only pill is safe (WHO Category 2) in migraine with aura and provides effective contraception without the oestrogen-related stroke risk.
The combined contraceptive patch contains oestrogen and is equally contraindicated (WHO Category 4) in migraine with aura — route of delivery does not mitigate the risk.
The combined vaginal ring also contains oestrogen and is equally contraindicated (WHO Category 4) in migraine with aura.
Lowering the oestrogen dose does not reduce the stroke risk in migraine with aura; all oestrogen-containing methods remain absolutely contraindicated.
clinicalPearl: Migraine with aura is a WHO Category 4 (absolute contraindication) for ALL combined hormonal contraceptives regardless of oestrogen dose or delivery route — use progestogen-only methods instead.
keyClue: Migraine WITH aura — the critical distinction that makes all oestrogen-containing contraceptives absolutely contraindicated.
commonExamTrap: Thinking that lowering the oestrogen dose or switching to a patch/ring makes combined contraception safe in migraine with aura — it does not.
missedConcept: The stroke risk in migraine with aura is a class effect of oestrogen in all CHC formulations, not a dose-dependent effect.

clinicalStem: A 36-year-old woman with gestational diabetes managed on metformin is in active labour at 39 weeks. Fetal weight is estimated at 4.4 kg on the third trimester scan. Following delivery of the fetal head, the midwife calls for urgent help — the head retracts against the maternal perineum and routine traction has failed to deliver the shoulders. The senior obstetrician arrives within 60 seconds. What is the single most important immediate action?
options: Apply fundal pressure to assist delivery of the anterior shoulder
Call the neonatology team and prepare for neonatal resuscitation
McRoberts manoeuvre (hyperflexion of maternal thighs) combined with suprapubic pressure
Proceed immediately to Zavanelli manoeuvre (cephalic replacement) and emergency caesarean
Apply lateral neck traction to free the impacted shoulder
explanation: Shoulder dystocia is an obstetric emergency requiring a structured, systematic response. The first-line manoeuvre is McRoberts (maternal thigh hyperflexion to flatten lumbar lordosis and widen the pelvic outlet) combined with suprapubic pressure applied by an assistant. Together these resolve approximately 50–60% of shoulder dystocias. Fundal pressure is absolutely contraindicated as it worsens impaction. Zavanelli is a last-resort option after all other manoeuvres fail.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Fundal pressure is absolutely contraindicated in shoulder dystocia — it drives the anterior shoulder deeper into the symphysis pubis and worsens the impaction.
Preparing the neonatal team is important but is a concurrent task, not the single most important immediate action; the priority is delivering the baby.
This is the correct answer: McRoberts manoeuvre combined with suprapubic pressure is the universally recommended first-line intervention for shoulder dystocia.
The Zavanelli manoeuvre is a last resort used only after all other manoeuvres (McRoberts, suprapubic pressure, internal rotation manoeuvres, posterior arm delivery) have failed.
Excessive lateral neck traction risks brachial plexus injury (Erb's palsy); controlled axial traction downward is appropriate, but the primary intervention remains McRoberts plus suprapubic pressure.
clinicalPearl: In shoulder dystocia, McRoberts manoeuvre plus suprapubic pressure is the first-line response; fundal pressure is absolutely contraindicated and will worsen the impaction.
keyClue: Turtle sign (head retracts against perineum) after head delivery — the hallmark of shoulder dystocia.
commonExamTrap: Applying fundal pressure in shoulder dystocia — a common instinct that is absolutely wrong and dangerous.
missedConcept: The structured HELPERR algorithm for shoulder dystocia; McRoberts and suprapubic pressure are always first before any internal manoeuvres.
redFlagReason: Shoulder dystocia is a time-critical obstetric emergency: prolonged head-to-body delivery interval causes fetal hypoxia, permanent brachial plexus injury, and death. Incorrect manoeuvres (fundal pressure) directly worsen the impaction.

sourceLecture: Early and Late Fetal Growth Assessment & Well being
sourceFile: Early and Late Fetal Growth Assessment & Well being.pdf
clinicalStem: A 32-year-old primigravida at 32 weeks gestation is referred following a routine growth scan showing an abdominal circumference on the 3rd centile with an estimated fetal weight of 1.38 kg. Amniotic fluid index is 7 cm (low-normal). Umbilical artery Doppler shows absent end-diastolic flow. The patient is haemodynamically stable and reports normal fetal movements. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Repeat growth scan in 2 weeks and continue community midwife-led care
Admit to hospital, commence corticosteroids, and arrange daily cardiotocography with umbilical artery Doppler
Perform a biophysical profile score and if ≥8/10, discharge with outpatient follow-up
Proceed to immediate delivery by caesarean section regardless of gestation
Increase maternal protein intake and refer to dietitian for nutritional optimisation
explanation: Absent end-diastolic flow (AEDF) on umbilical artery Doppler is a marker of severely elevated placental vascular resistance and significant fetal compromise, indicating fetal growth restriction at a critical stage. At 32 weeks, the appropriate management is hospital admission for close surveillance (daily CTG and Doppler), administration of antenatal corticosteroids (to promote fetal lung maturity in preparation for likely preterm delivery), and multidisciplinary planning. Immediate delivery is not always required if daily monitoring is reassuring, but the severity of AEDF mandates inpatient care.
wrongAnswerExplanations: A 2-week interval and community care are wholly inadequate for AEDF, which requires immediate inpatient surveillance — this approach risks unexpected fetal demise.
This is the correct answer: AEDF at 32 weeks requires admission, corticosteroids for lung maturity, and intensive surveillance; delivery timing is guided by subsequent Doppler and CTG findings.
A single reassuring biophysical profile is insufficient to safely discharge a fetus with AEDF; the placental compromise is ongoing and deterioration can be rapid.
Immediate delivery at 32 weeks carries significant neonatal morbidity; delivery should be considered if Doppler deteriorates to reversed flow, CTG becomes abnormal, or other acute indicators arise.
Nutritional intervention does not address the uteroplacental vascular pathology underlying AEDF and is not an evidence-based response to this emergency finding.
clinicalPearl: Absent end-diastolic flow on umbilical artery Doppler mandates immediate inpatient management with corticosteroids and intensive surveillance — it is a pre-terminal Doppler finding requiring preparation for preterm delivery.
keyClue: Absent end-diastolic flow on umbilical artery Doppler at 32 weeks — the critical surveillance finding.
commonExamTrap: Choosing immediate delivery without considering that corticosteroids and stabilisation should precede delivery unless acute deterioration demands immediate action.
missedConcept: AEDF represents critical uteroplacental insufficiency; the management hierarchy is admission → steroids → intensive monitoring → timed delivery, not immediate caesarean.
redFlagReason: Absent end-diastolic flow is a pre-terminal Doppler finding associated with a high risk of in-utero fetal demise; inadequate surveillance can result in preventable stillbirth.

clinicalStem: A 29-year-old woman with a levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine system (LNG-IUS, Mirena) in situ for 2 years presents with a 3-week history of lower abdominal pain predominantly on the right side, light vaginal bleeding, and a missed period. Her last menstrual period was 6 weeks ago. A urine pregnancy test is positive. On examination, she is haemodynamically stable; there is right adnexal tenderness. A transvaginal ultrasound shows no intrauterine pregnancy; the right adnexa contains a 2.2 cm heterogeneous mass. What is the most important risk factor for her current presentation?
options: The LNG-IUS has failed to prevent implantation and the pregnancy is intrauterine
She has developed an endometrioma due to the LNG-IUS
She has a right ovarian cyst unrelated to her positive pregnancy test
When a pregnancy occurs with any IUD in situ, it is significantly more likely to be ectopic than intrauterine
The LNG-IUS has caused a corpus luteum cyst and false-positive pregnancy test
explanation: Although intrauterine devices (both copper and LNG-IUS) dramatically reduce the absolute risk of pregnancy, when pregnancy does occur in a woman with an IUD in situ, the likelihood of it being ectopic is substantially higher than in the general pregnant population — approximately 50% or more of IUD failures are ectopic. The IUD effectively prevents intrauterine implantation but is less effective at preventing tubal implantation. This patient's presentation (positive pregnancy test + no intrauterine pregnancy + adnexal mass) is consistent with ectopic pregnancy.
wrongAnswerExplanations: The ultrasound clearly shows no intrauterine pregnancy, making this option incorrect.
An endometrioma would not produce a positive pregnancy test; the positive test confirms the adnexal mass is pregnancy-related.
A corpus luteum cyst can coexist with pregnancy but cannot explain a heterogeneous adnexal mass in the context of a positive pregnancy test and no intrauterine pregnancy — ectopic must be excluded.

This is the correct answer: when pregnancy occurs despite any IUD, the proportion of those pregnancies that are ectopic is disproportionately high because the IUD prevents intrauterine but not tubal implantation.
A urine pregnancy test becomes positive through beta-hCG; a corpus luteum cyst does not cause a false-positive pregnancy test.
clinicalPearl: Any pregnancy occurring with an IUD in situ must be presumed ectopic until proven otherwise — the IUD prevents intrauterine implantation more effectively than tubal implantation.
keyClue: Positive pregnancy test + IUD in situ + no intrauterine pregnancy on ultrasound + adnexal mass.
commonExamTrap: Assuming that because the IUD is very effective, a positive pregnancy test with IUD in situ must be a false positive.
missedConcept: IUDs reduce absolute pregnancy risk greatly but significantly shift the proportion of any conceived pregnancies toward being ectopic.

subtopic: Valproate teratogenicity and neural tube defects
clinicalStem: A 24-year-old woman with juvenile myoclonic epilepsy managed on sodium valproate 1000 mg daily attends her GP for pre-conception counselling. She is planning her first pregnancy. Her epilepsy has been well-controlled for 3 years with no seizures. She asks whether she needs to change her medication. What is the most appropriate management recommendation?
options: Continue sodium valproate as seizure control takes priority over teratogenic risk
Switch to lamotrigine or levetiracetam after specialist neurology review; prescribe high-dose folic acid 5 mg daily
Switch her immediately to phenytoin, which is safer in pregnancy
Stop all antiepileptic medication and manage seizures acutely if they occur during pregnancy
Continue valproate and prescribe standard 400 mcg folic acid to reduce neural tube risk
explanation: Sodium valproate has the highest teratogenic risk of all antiepileptic drugs, causing major congenital malformations in 6–11% of exposed fetuses (neural tube defects, cardiac defects, hypospadias) and fetal valproate syndrome (characteristic facial features, developmental delay). It is also associated with a 30–40% risk of neurodevelopmental disorders in exposed children. MHRA guidance mandates that valproate should not be used in women of childbearing potential unless a Pregnancy Prevention Programme is in place. Pre-conceptually, switching to lamotrigine or levetiracetam under specialist supervision is the appropriate pathway, alongside high-dose folic acid 5 mg.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Valproate's teratogenic and neurodevelopmental risks are so severe that maintaining it through pregnancy without review is not justifiable; alternative AEDs provide adequate seizure control with far lower fetal risk.

This is the correct answer: specialist review to switch to lamotrigine or levetiracetam (safer AED profiles in pregnancy) combined with high-dose folic acid pre-conceptually is the recommended management.
Phenytoin is also a known teratogen (fetal hydantoin syndrome) and is not recommended as a safer alternative to valproate in this context.
Abrupt discontinuation of antiepileptic medication without specialist input risks uncontrolled seizures, status epilepticus, and maternal/fetal hypoxia — this is dangerous and unacceptable.

Standard 400 mcg folic acid is insufficient for women on valproate who are at very high risk of NTDs; 5 mg daily is required, and more critically, the valproate itself must be reviewed and ideally changed.
clinicalPearl: Sodium valproate has the highest fetal teratogenic risk of all antiepileptics and should be avoided in women planning pregnancy; switch to lamotrigine or levetiracetam under specialist supervision and prescribe 5 mg folic acid pre-conceptually.
keyClue: Sodium valproate + pre-conception counselling + planning pregnancy — the combination mandating medication review.
commonExamTrap: Prescribing only high-dose folic acid without addressing the valproate itself — folic acid reduces NTD risk but does not negate valproate's full teratogenic profile.
missedConcept: Valproate's teratogenicity includes not just NTDs but also fetal valproate syndrome and significant neurodevelopmental harm; medication change before conception is mandatory.

sourceLecture: Early and Late Fetal Growth Assessment & Well being
sourceFile: Early and Late Fetal Growth Assessment & Well being.pdf
subtopic: First trimester combined screening for Down syndrome
clinicalStem: A 38-year-old woman presents at 11 weeks + 4 days for her booking antenatal appointment. She is concerned about chromosomal abnormalities due to her age. She is offered first trimester combined screening. Which combination of tests constitutes first trimester combined screening for trisomy 21?
options: Maternal serum AFP and urine estriol measured at 11–14 weeks
Nuchal translucency ultrasound measurement alone at 11–14 weeks
Nuchal translucency ultrasound + maternal serum free beta-hCG + PAPP-A, adjusted for maternal age, at 11–14 weeks
Cell-free fetal DNA (cfDNA) analysis of maternal blood, which is the standard first-line screen
Morphology ultrasound scan at 11–14 weeks to detect structural markers of trisomy 21
explanation: First trimester combined screening (FTCS) integrates: (1) nuchal translucency (NT) measurement by ultrasound at 11+0 to 13+6 weeks, (2) maternal serum free beta-hCG, and (3) pregnancy-associated plasma protein A (PAPP-A) — all adjusted for maternal age and gestational age. This combination achieves a detection rate of approximately 85–90% for trisomy 21 with a 5% false-positive rate. Cell-free DNA is not the standard first-line screen in most national programmes but may be offered as a contingency or second-tier test.
wrongAnswerExplanations: AFP and urine estriol are components of second trimester quadruple screening (triple/quad test), not first trimester combined screening.
NT measurement alone (without biochemistry) provides approximately 70–75% detection for trisomy 21 — acceptable but inferior to the full combined screen.
This is the correct answer: NT + free beta-hCG + PAPP-A + maternal age adjustment is the standard first trimester combined screen with ~85–90% detection rate for Down syndrome.
cfDNA (NIPT) has higher sensitivity (>99%) but in most national programmes it is a second-tier test offered after a high-risk combined screen result, not the primary screen.
The 11–14 week scan assesses NT and confirms viability/gestation but is not a morphology scan; detailed anatomy assessment occurs at the 18–20 week anomaly scan.
clinicalPearl: First trimester combined screening = NT ultrasound + maternal serum free beta-hCG + PAPP-A, all adjusted for maternal age — achieving approximately 85–90% detection of trisomy 21.
keyClue: 11 weeks + 4 days gestation — within the 11+0 to 13+6 week window for first trimester combined screening.
commonExamTrap: Confusing first trimester (NT + beta-hCG + PAPP-A) with second trimester screening (AFP + estriol + hCG ± inhibin A).
missedConcept: PAPP-A is the key biochemical marker specific to first trimester combined screening; it falls in trisomy 21 pregnancies.

clinicalStem: A 17-year-old woman with a BMI of 19 kg/m² requests long-acting contraception. She is a non-smoker with no significant medical history. She plays competitive sport and is concerned about fertility preservation. She is offered depot medroxyprogesterone acetate (DMPA, Depo-Provera). Which of the following is the most clinically significant concern specific to DMPA in this patient that should be discussed before prescribing?
options: Irreversible infertility after use of DMPA for more than 12 months
High risk of thromboembolic events due to progestogen effect
Significant and potentially prolonged reduction in bone mineral density, particularly concerning in an adolescent athlete
DMPA is absolutely contraindicated in women under 18 years of age
Acne and virilisation due to androgenic side effects of depot progestogen
explanation: DMPA causes a significant, dose-dependent reduction in bone mineral density (BMD) that is greatest during the period of use and partially recovers after discontinuation. This is particularly concerning in adolescents who are still accumulating peak bone mass — a time when BMD losses may not fully recover, with potential long-term osteoporosis risk. UK guidelines suggest that in adolescents under 18, DMPA should be used only when other methods are unacceptable, and a review at 2 years should assess whether ongoing use is appropriate. Athletes already at risk of the Female Athlete Triad make this concern even more relevant.
wrongAnswerExplanations: DMPA does cause a delay in return of fertility (median 10 months after last injection) but this is temporary, not irreversible — long-term fertility is not affected.
DMPA is not associated with a significant increase in thromboembolic risk (unlike oestrogen-containing contraceptives); this is not the primary counselling point.

This is the correct answer: BMD reduction during DMPA use is well established and is especially significant in adolescents who are still building peak bone mass, and even more so in a competitive athlete.
DMPA is not absolutely contraindicated under 18; UK guidance says it should be used with caution and annual review in adolescents, not avoided entirely.
While DMPA can cause skin changes, significant virilisation is not a recognised side effect — androgenic effects with DMPA are minimal compared to older depot formulations.
clinicalPearl: DMPA causes dose-dependent bone mineral density reduction; in adolescents still accumulating peak bone mass, this is the key concern and UK guidance recommends a 2-year review of ongoing use.
keyClue: 17-year-old competitive athlete + DMPA request — combination of peak bone mass accrual period and athletic activity amplifies BMD concern.
commonExamTrap: Stating that DMPA causes permanent infertility — fertility return is delayed but not permanently impaired.
missedConcept: Peak bone mass is accumulated during adolescence; DMPA-induced BMD loss during this period may not be fully recoverable, making age and athletic status critical counselling points.
